stories.thread: Blue Bell Broken Arrow Plant Contamination and Shutdown
stories.story: Blue Bell ice cream temporarily shut down its Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, manufacturing plant after listeria contamination was discovered in a serving of ice cream from that plant. Public health officials warned consumers not to eat any Blue Bell-branded products made there, including 3-ounce servings for institutions marked with O, P, Q, R, S, or T behind the coding date. The company closed the facility "out of an abundance of caution" to search for the contamination cause. A contaminated cup produced at the Broken Arrow plant in April 2014 was recovered from a Kansas hospital, and listeria bacteria was also found in additional samples from the plant. The bacteria in the hospital and factory samples appeared to match each other genetically.
key_facts: Blue Bell Broken Arrow plant shut down
Listeria contamination discovered in ice cream serving
Public health officials warned consumers not to eat products from Broken Arrow plant
Specific 3-ounce servings for institutions (O, P, Q, R, S, T) included in warning
Plant shut down "out of an abundance of caution"
Contaminated cup from April 2014 produced at Broken Arrow plant
Listeria found in additional samples from the plant
Bacteria in hospital and factory samples matched genetically

stories.thread: Blue Bell Product Recalls and Company History
stories.story: Blue Bell has recalled products multiple times, marking the first product recall in the 108-year history of Blue Bell Creameries. In early March, the company recalled a group of products made at a Texas plant due to the Kansas listeria outbreak, later adding 3-ounce cup servings to this recall. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's warning about the Broken Arrow plant does not affect other Blue Bell ice cream not made at that specific plant. Blue Bell stated that this recall in no way includes Blue Bell ice cream half gallons, pints, quarts, 3 gallons, or other 3 oz. cups.
key_facts: First product recall in 108-year history of Blue Bell Creameries
Blue Bell recalled other products
Early March recall of products from a Texas plant due to Kansas listeria outbreak
3-ounce cup servings later added to the recall
Warning does not affect other Blue Bell ice cream not made at the Broken Arrow plant
Recall does not include half gallons, pints, quarts, 3 gallons, or other 3 oz. cups

stories.story: Five people were infected and three died in the past year in Kansas from listeria that might be linked to Blue Bell Creameries products. All five were hospitalized at the same hospital before developing listeriosis, and at least four had consumed milkshakes made with Blue Bell ice cream. Listeria monocytogenes was found in a cup of ice cream recovered from the hospital, and genetically matched bacteria found in samples from the Broken Arrow plant. However, these samples did not appear identical to listeria samples taken from patients infected in the Kansas outbreak. The CDC states that investigation is ongoing to determine whether these illnesses are related to exposure to Blue Bell products, and it is not clear if the ice cream is the source of the infections.
key_facts: Five people infected and three died in Kansas in the past year from listeria
Listeria might be linked to Blue Bell Creameries products
All five hospitalized at the same hospital before developing listeriosis
At least four consumed milkshakes made with Blue Bell ice cream
Listeria monocytogenes found in a cup of ice cream from the hospital
Hospital sample genetically matched factory sample from Broken Arrow plant
Hospital/factory samples did not appear identical to listeria from Kansas patients
Investigation is ongoing to determine if illnesses are related to Blue Bell products
explicit_unknowns: It is not clear if the ice cream is the source of the infections.

stories.story: In a separate outbreak in Texas, the CDC found that listeria samples taken from patients who developed listeriosis between 2010 and 2014 matched listeria in recovered samples. These recovered samples were from a hospital that served 3-ounce Blue Bell cups. The CDC states that investigation is ongoing to determine whether these illnesses are related to exposure to Blue Bell products. It is not clear if the ice cream is the source of these infections.
key_facts: Separate listeria outbreak in Texas
Listeria samples from patients (2010-2014) matched recovered samples
Hospital that served 3-ounce Blue Bell cups had recovered samples
Investigation is ongoing to determine if illnesses are related to Blue Bell products
explicit_unknowns: It is not clear if the ice cream is the source of these infections.

stories.thread: Blue Bell's Response and Public Health Advice
stories.story: Blue Bell CEO and president Paul Kruse stated the company is "devastated" and "know that Blue Bell has to be and can be better than this." He emphasized that "Quality and safety have always been our top priorities" and expressed deep sadness and concern for all those affected. The CDC advises individuals and institutions to check their freezers for the recalled products and throw them away.
key_facts: Blue Bell CEO Paul Kruse statement: "devastated," "can be better"
CEO stated "Quality and safety have always been our top priorities"
CEO expressed deep sadness and concern for those affected
CDC advises individuals and institutions to check freezers for recalled products
CDC advises throwing away recalled products

stories.story: Listeriosis is a serious infection caused by eating food contaminated with listeria, primarily affecting the elderly, pregnant women, newborns, and people with weakened immune systems. Symptoms include fever and muscle aches, sometimes associated with diarrhea or other gastrointestinal symptoms. In the United States, an estimated 1,600 people become seriously ill each year, and approximately 16% of these illnesses result in death. Cervical infections caused by listeriosis in pregnant women may result in stillbirth or spontaneous abortion during the second or third trimesters.
key_facts: Listeriosis is a serious infection
Caused by eating food contaminated with listeria
Primarily affects elderly, pregnant women, newborns, and people with weakened immune systems
Symptoms: fever, muscle aches, sometimes diarrhea or other gastrointestinal symptoms
Estimated 1,600 people become seriously ill each year in the United States
Approximately 16% of these illnesses result in death
Cervical infections in pregnant women may result in stillbirth or spontaneous abortion during second or third trimesters
